title: Wet felting a flat panel
subtitle: The foundational technique every other felting project builds on
excerpt: Lay merino roving in cross-hatched layers, wet down with hot soapy water, and rub the surface until the fibres lock together into a firm flat panel. The technique underpins every other wet-felting project from bowls to hats.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the cross-cultural tradition of wet felting: Mongolian yurt-cover construction, Anatolian rug-felt, and the British and Scandinavian hat-felting trades. The cross-hatch layering method and the olive-oil soap approach are the standard kitchen-table technique taught consistently across modern felting guilds and introductory workshops. No single PD text documents this exact method; the substance is observation-led.

content.text: A flat felt panel is where
wet felting
starts. A small piece of merino
roving

, a bar of olive-oil soap, hot water, and a kitchen surface: in 30 to 45 minutes the fibres lock together into a firm, stable fabric. Every subsequent felting project, from bowls to hats to needle-felted pictures, uses this same foundation. Learn the physical signs of each stage here, and the rest follows.

attrs.body: Test hot water temperature on the back of your wrist before submerging hands. Allow the water to cool if it stings.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: What you need

items.qty: enough to wet a bamboo mat
items.name: Hot water (60-70°C)
Bamboo rolling mat or sushi mat

items.qty: piece large enough to cover the layout
items.name: Felting net or tulle (optional)
Bubble wrap (optional, as a textured surface)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Lay the first layer of roving

Lay the bamboo mat flat on a waterproof surface. Pull lengths of roving from the end of the ball and lay them side by side, slightly overlapping, in one direction across the mat. Cover an area roughly 25 x 25 cm (the felt will shrink to about 18 x 18 cm when finished). Keep the thickness even: too thin in one spot and the felt tears there; too thick and the centre stays unfelted.
Step 2: Add the second layer at 90 degrees
Lay a second layer of roving at right angles to the first, covering the same area. This
cross-hatched layers

arrangement is the key to a panel that doesn't stretch or tear in one direction: each layer locks the other in place as the fibres full. Three or four alternating layers are standard for a firm, thick panel. Two layers produce a thin, flexible felt; five or more produce a very thick, stiff felt.
Step 3: Wet down the layout

If you have a felting net, lay it over the dry roving to protect the surface from disturbing during the initial wetting. Rub the soap bar vigorously across the net (or directly across the roving if you are working without the net) to leave a layer of soapy residue on the surface. Then pour or sprinkle hot water over the layout until the roving is saturated. Press it gently flat with both hands. The soap lubricates the fibres and helps them move past each other as you rub; without soap, the fibres grip too early and the surface tears.
Step 4: Rub the surface for 10-15 minutes

Rub the wet surface with flat palms in straight passes: up and down the length, then across the width. Use moderate pressure at first and increase as the fibres begin to bind. After 5 minutes, the surface should feel less slippery and the roving should hold together when you lift a corner. After 10 minutes, the fibres should be starting to pull against your hands as you rub, which is the early sign of
fulling
. If the surface is slipping apart or separating into separate tufts, the water is too cold or there is not enough soap: re-soap and add hotter water.
Step 5: Pinch test

After 10 minutes, test the felt: lift a corner and pinch a small amount of the surface between finger and thumb. If individual fibres come away, the felt is not set yet. Continue rubbing. If you can lift only a matted layer (no loose fibres pulling free), the felt has formed enough to move to the rolling stage. This test is the most reliable guide to readiness. The visual appearance of the surface is a less reliable indicator: felt can look unified before it is structurally set.
Step 6: Roll and full

Roll the felt up inside the bamboo mat, jellyroll-style. Roll it backwards and forwards across the surface, pressing down firmly, for 2 minutes. Unroll, turn the felt 90 degrees, re-roll, and repeat. The rolling agitation tightens the fibre locks and shrinks the panel. After two rounds of rolling, the panel should be noticeably firmer and smaller. Continue rolling in all four orientations until the felt has reached the density and size you want.
Step 7: Rinse and finish

Rinse the finished felt panel under cool running water to remove the soap. The sudden temperature drop gives the fibres a final shock that helps stabilise the structure. Squeeze out the water (do not wring, which distorts the panel), press it flat between two dry tea towels, and lay it flat to dry. The panel will be slightly damp for several hours. Once dry, it is stable and washable.
Care and storage

Finished flat felt panels wash by hand in cool water with a gentle wool wash. Do not machine-wash: agitation continues the felting process and the panel will shrink further and lose its shape. Press dry between towels and lay flat; do not hang a damp panel, as the weight distorts it. Felt panels store flat in a dry space away from moths. Cedar balls or lavender sachets deter moths without the chemical residue of mothballs.

attrs.intro: The three most frequent failures in a first flat-panel session, and what to do.

items.fix: Re-soap the surface and add freshly boiled water. If the roving is labelled above 22-micron, add another 5-10 minutes of rubbing before the pinch test.
items.cause: Water too cold, not enough soap, or the fibres are an uncommonly coarse merino grade that needs more working time.
items.symptom: Fibres pulling apart when you lift a corner after 10 minutes

items.fix: Use lighter pressure for the first 5 minutes. Let the soap and heat do the initial binding work before increasing pressure.
items.cause: Too much pressure too early, before the fibres have bonded enough to hold against friction.

items.fix: When laying roving, hold each length at both ends and pull it to a consistent thickness before placing it. A few minutes spent on the layout stage saves all the corrective work later.
items.cause: Uneven layer thickness during the layout stage.
items.symptom: Uneven thickness in the finished panel

attrs.heading: Common problems
